user_query:
I’m traveling from a landlocked country to this coastal town to explore its maritime history, and I’ve just had two preparatory meetings. Please help me turn my notes into a useful plan and follow-ups.

Inputs (read all of these):
- input/notes_museum_meeting.md
- input/notes_tour_briefing.md
- input/attractions.csv

Deliverables and rules:
1) Consolidated meeting summary
- Create output/meeting_summary.md that:
  - Summarizes each meeting separately (date/time, attendees, key points, decisions, and explicit action items).
  - Includes an overall “Action Items” checklist that merges deduplicated tasks across meetings.
  - Ends with a “Cross-check” section listing the top 3 ranked attractions (by the scoring in step 2) with their scores and matched topic words.

2) Ranked attractions list (filter, sort, and rank)
- Extract my priority topics from the notes: lowercased keywords or short phrases that reflect interests I explicitly expressed or that were recommended to me (e.g., lighthouse, shipwreck, harbor pilots, port commerce, signal station, coastal defense) based on the actual note content.
- Save these to output/priority_topics.json as an array of objects with fields: topic (string) and source_file (string: which note file it came from).
- Use these topics to score and rank attractions from input/attractions.csv with the following deterministic rules:
  - Only consider rows where category == "maritime".
  - Treat the tags column as semicolon-separated tokens. Matching is case-insensitive.
  - Split multi-word topics into individual words (e.g., "harbor pilots" -> ["harbor", "pilots"]). A tag matches a topic word if the topic word appears as a full token or substring of the tag (case-insensitive).
  - Scoring: +2 points for each unique topic word matched in the attraction’s tags (count each topic word at most once per attraction), plus +1 point if the recommended_by field equals the full name of any contact extracted from the notes (e.g., “Dr. Marina Cole”, “Alex Tan”).
  - Sort by descending score, then ascending name for ties.
- Save the ranked list to output/attractions_ranked.csv with columns: id, name, category, score, matched_topics, recommended_by. matched_topics should be a semicolon-separated list of the distinct topic words that contributed to the score for that attraction.

3) Draft two follow-up emails (plain text)
- Create output/emails/marina_cole_followup.txt and output/emails/alex_tan_followup.txt. Each should include a Subject line, a greeting with the contact’s name, and a concise body that references the relevant meeting and requests.
  - marina_cole_followup.txt (to mcole@coastalmuseum.org): confirm the reading-room visit on Tuesday at 2:00 PM; state I will send an ID scan by 2026-05-05; request the finding aid for port commerce archive boxes; ask whether photography is permitted in the lighthouse lens gallery.
  - alex_tan_followup.txt (to alex.tan@harborwalks.org): confirm availability for the Friday morning 9:00 AM tour; request the exact meeting point and any weather considerations; mention my interest in harbor pilots oral histories and coastal defense battery areas.

General constraints:
- Use only the provided files. Do not invent facts not present in the notes.
- Make the ranking strictly follow the scoring rules above so results are reproducible.
- Keep emails as plain text files (no HTML).

Output paths to produce:
- output/meeting_summary.md
- output/priority_topics.json
- output/attractions_ranked.csv
- output/emails/marina_cole_followup.txt
- output/emails/alex_tan_followup.txt All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
